sections.context: Burrow V1 is the OS-isolated sandbox runtime layer for the os-eco coding-agent ecosystem (greenhouse → overstory → sapling/claude-code). Today, agents either run on the host (polluting toolchains, risky filesystem access) or inside Docker (heavyweight, slow). Burrow wraps native OS sandbox primitives — bwrap on Linux, sandbox-exec on macOS — so any CLI-based coding agent runs in a workspace-scoped jail with declared network policy and explicit env passthrough. V1 is local-first and single-user; the BurrowProvider and AgentRuntime seams are designed so remote substrates (fly/AWS) and new agents land as additions, not rewrites. This epic decomposes the work per SPEC.md §22.
sections.approach: Implement V1 in 11 sequential phases (0-10) per SPEC.md §22, totaling ~3-4k LOC over 12-16 focused days. Each phase produces a working slice end-to-end against its scope so we can verify SPEC.md §23 success criteria incrementally. Architecture is Bun + TypeScript strict, citty CLI on top of a typed Client library, drizzle-on-bun:sqlite for durable state, p-queue for per-burrow concurrency. Native sandbox wrappers (no srt, no Docker) so Burrow owns the contract. Two seams kept first-class: BurrowProvider (LocalProvider in V1, FlyProvider stub) and AgentRuntime (claude-code, sapling, codex built-ins plus declarative AgentConfig). Each phase becomes a child seed that can be sd plan submit'd individually when picked up — this top-level plan deliberately stops at phase granularity to avoid freezing detail before we learn from earlier phases.

alternatives.name: Use Anthropic's srt (sandbox-runtime) wrapper instead of writing native bwrap/sandbox-exec
alternatives.rejected_because: Adds a third-party CLI dependency in the critical path of daily workflow and gives up ownership of the profile contract. Future provider implementations (Fly, AWS) need to generate equivalent profiles directly anyway. Native primitives are stable OS interfaces; ~500-800 LOC that changes rarely once correct (SPEC.md §8.6).

alternatives.name: Docker-based isolation
alternatives.rejected_because: OS-level sandbox primitives are dramatically lighter than containers, with no image management, no daemon, no per-burrow pull. Burrow is explicitly not a container manager (SPEC.md §2.3).

alternatives.name: Build remote provider (Fly) in V1 alongside LocalProvider
alternatives.rejected_because: Doubles scope. The BurrowProvider seam earns its keep regardless; shipping the second implementation can be additive post-V1 (SPEC.md §3.2).

alternatives.name: Postgres + Redis + HTTP server from day one
alternatives.rejected_because: V1 is local-first and single-user — SQLite WAL handles durable state in-process, no daemon, no external services. Reversibility for future server modes is preserved by drizzle's multi-dialect support (SPEC.md §6).

alternatives.name: Big-bang implementation rather than 11 phases
alternatives.rejected_because: Each phase produces a verifiable slice tied to SPEC.md §23 success criteria. Phasing also means quality gates run continuously and we discover spec gaps early.

steps.title: Phase 0 — Scaffold the Bun/TypeScript project skeleton
Phase 1 — Native sandbox wrappers (bwrap + sandbox-exec) with SSH passthrough and network policy
Phase 2 — SQLite data model, drizzle schema, repos, and per-burrow run loop with crash recovery
Phase 3 — Workspace materialization: git worktree for project burrows, fork for task burrows
Phase 4 — AgentRuntime interface + built-ins (claude-code, sapling, codex) + declarative AgentConfig
Phase 5 — Inbox + steering: messages table, send/chat commands, next-turn injection
Phase 6 — Event tail + archive: in-memory pub/sub, logs/events --follow, NDJSON archiver on destroy
Phase 7 — Library API + full CLI surface: Client class, all commands wired, JSON/pretty modes, branding pass
Phase 8 — burrow.toml schema, secrets resolution, toolchain doctor (with op:// 1Password support)
Phase 9 — burrow ship: build + deploy artifacts with fly and render as first ShipTargets
Phase 10 — Polish: README, examples, error message review, branding compliance, shell completions

risks: bwrap and sandbox-exec have subtly different capability models; profile drift between Linux and macOS could let one platform succeed where the other silently leaks. Mitigation: shared SandboxProfile contract validated by integration tests on both platforms.

git worktree against the host clone gives the agent read-write access to .git/, so a misbehaving agent could corrupt host git metadata. Mitigation: read_only_main_branch flag plus path-policy constraints (SPEC.md §11.2).

Spawn-per-turn vs one-shot runtimes diverge enough that the inbox-injection abstraction may leak. Mitigation: encodeInboxMessage hook on the runtime + clear CLI surfacing when targeting a one-shot runtime (SPEC.md §13.3).

Network policy enforcement on Linux: userspace HTTP proxy vs nftables. The latter is more correct but less portable. Decision deferred per open question §25.3 — ship userspace proxy first, nftables opt-in later.

Drizzle is the only abstraction layered on bun:sqlite. If drizzle's bun:sqlite adapter regresses, we may hit blockers. Mitigation: keep schema and queries simple enough that we could drop to raw bun:sqlite if needed.

Toolchain mounting strategy (specific binary vs full $PATH ancestry) interacts badly with shim-based managers (mise, asdf, fnm). Decision deferred per open question §25.4 — V1 mounts resolved binary + lib dir, shim-aware mode via burrow.toml flag.

Claude Code credential cache forwarding is convenience-critical but couples us to Anthropic's auth layout. Mitigation: detect-and-skip-on-failure rather than hard requirement; user can always log in inside burrow.
acceptance: burrow up in a project with a burrow.toml succeeds and agents run inside an isolated sandbox with the host filesystem outside the workspace unreachable (SPEC.md §23).
Four parallel burrows (one project + three task burrows) can run agents simultaneously without races.
kill -9 on the burrow process leaves the system recoverable: in-flight runs marked failed, burrows still listed, workspaces intact.
burrow send <id> "<msg>" is delivered on the next turn for claude-code and sapling runtimes.
burrow events --follow interleaves events from all active burrows in real-time as NDJSON.
burrow destroy <id> archives the burrow's complete event log to ${dataDir}/archive/<id>/events.jsonl.
Adding a Gemini-CLI-shaped agent requires only a [[agents]] entry in burrow.toml — zero code changes in src/.
burrow doctor correctly identifies a missing host toolchain declared in burrow.toml and refuses to up until fixed.
A new IC running `bun install -g @os-eco/burrow-cli && cd project && burrow up` is productive without further setup.

A future FlyProvider can be added without modifying any file under src/core/, src/db/, src/runtime/, src/inbox/, src/events/, or src/runner/ — the seam holds (the load-bearing test of the architecture).
bun test && bun run lint && bun run typecheck pass cleanly across the whole codebase at the end of every phase.

sections.context: burrow watch is the first post-V1 feature per SPEC §24 (immediately ahead of burrow serve and the future web UI). The naive approach is to write a self-contained TUI that pokes ANSI codes inline against repos/event-bus queries (overstory's dashboard.ts is shaped that way). The opportunity is to build it as the pre-web-UI: a stable DashboardSnapshot view-model is the load-bearing seam, and today it drives a terminal renderer while tomorrow it drives a React frontend over burrow serve. Same shape, two faces. This mirrors the precedent already in the spec — the §14.1 NDJSON event envelope is the exact same pattern for events. Building the snapshot contract now means burrow serve is a thin wrapper later, not a rewrite, and we gain a --json mode immediately that scripts/CI can consume.
sections.approach: Layer the feature so the view-model (data) is decoupled from the renderer (presentation). Three files own the contract: src/dashboard/types.ts (DashboardSnapshot, BurrowCard, RunSummary, EventTailEntry shapes — additive-only versioning convention same as §14.1 events), src/dashboard/snapshot.ts (pure builder over Repos), and src/dashboard/stream.ts (async generator coalescing event-bus pushes + cooperative-abort polling fallback, mirroring the patterns already established in src/events/tail.ts + src/events/poll.ts). Two more files own the TUI face: src/dashboard/tui-state.ts (pure key-handling reducer over a viewState) and src/dashboard/tui-render.ts (pure renderSnapshot(snap, viewState, termSize): string returning the screen as one ANSI blob; golden-tested). The runtime (alt-screen, raw mode, resize, keypress dispatch) lives in src/dashboard/tui.ts and is the only impure piece. The CLI command parses args and chooses TUI vs --json mode; --json emits raw DashboardSnapshot NDJSON (the exact shape burrow serve will WebSocket-stream). Zero runtime deps beyond what burrow already pulls — raw ANSI like overstory but layered. Finally we lock the contract publicly: re-export types from the library entry and add SPEC §27 'Dashboard view model' documenting the envelope, the additive-only rule, and the burrow serve forward-compat plan.

alternatives.name: Adopt Ink (React for terminal)
alternatives.rejected_because: Heavy runtime dep and the goal is to feed a future React web frontend, not run React in the terminal. Raw ANSI keeps the terminal renderer thin and makes the view-model — not the renderer — the load-bearing seam. Conflating view-state and render also makes future web reuse harder, not easier.

alternatives.name: Use blessed / blessed-contrib
alternatives.rejected_because: Same heavy-dep / niche-maintenance reasons as Ink, and they conflate layout with rendering — exactly the seam we want to keep clean.

alternatives.name: Copy overstory's dashboard.ts shape (single file, ANSI inline)
alternatives.rejected_because: Works for a one-shot TUI but bakes the renderer into the data layer. When burrow serve lands, we'd have to re-derive the snapshot shape; until then we'd have no --json mode for scripts/CI. The decoupling cost is one extra file (snapshot.ts) and pays back the moment the web UI starts.

alternatives.name: Build burrow serve + React web UI first, skip the TUI
alternatives.rejected_because: SPEC §24 puts burrow watch ahead of burrow serve in roadmap order, and dogfooding parallel agent work is a TUI-shaped problem today. Designing the snapshot contract while building the TUI is what makes burrow serve cheap later — not the other way around.

alternatives.name: Ship only --json mode, defer the TUI
alternatives.rejected_because: Kills the immediate UX win and the 'first post-V1 feature' mandate. The TUI is also the forcing function that proves the snapshot contract supports a real renderer.

steps.title: Define DashboardSnapshot view-model types (src/dashboard/types.ts) and lock additive-only versioning convention
Implement pure buildSnapshot(repos): DashboardSnapshot builder with synthetic-DB unit tests
Implement streamSnapshots(repos, bus, opts) async generator — event-bus driven with coalescing + abort-aware polling fallback
Implement pure TUI view-state reducer (src/dashboard/tui-state.ts) for keypress → viewState transitions (q/j/k/enter/esc, scroll, focus)
Implement pure renderSnapshot(snap, viewState, termSize): string with golden tests covering layout + resize
Implement TUI runtime (src/dashboard/tui.ts): alt-screen entry/exit, raw-mode keypress dispatch, resize listener, snapshot-stream consumer, clean SIGINT shutdown
Wire burrow watch CLI command (src/cli/commands/watch.ts) — TUI mode plus --json NDJSON snapshot mode mirroring burrow events; register in src/cli/main.ts

Lock public surface: export DashboardSnapshot/buildSnapshot/streamSnapshots from src/index.ts; add SPEC §27 'Dashboard view model' (envelope, additive-only rule, burrow serve forward-compat); update CHANGELOG; bump to 0.2.0

risks: Bun raw-mode TTY input may have rough edges vs Node — verify keypress handling (especially modifier keys, paste, terminal resize signals) on both macOS and Linux during step 6 before locking the API.

Terminal resize race: a redraw mid-resize can produce torn output. Mitigation: debounce SIGWINCH and re-render only on the trailing edge; cover with a deterministic test that simulates resize via injected termSize.

Snapshot rebuild cost at high event volume — N burrows × M events/s could hammer SQLite. Mitigation: coalesce within a configurable window (default 50–100ms) and rebuild from scratch (cheap at V1 scale) rather than diffing.

View-model drift: once burrow serve ships, removing or renaming a field would break web clients. Lock additive-only in SPEC §27 from day one; don't ship anything optional that we're not committed to. Treat the snapshot envelope with the same discipline as §14.1 events.

TTY-detection mismatch with --json: defaulting to TUI when stdout is a TTY but JSON when piped (mirroring burrow events) is the right behavior but easy to break. Add a CLI test that asserts both branches.

Detail-pane scrollback memory: holding all events for a focused burrow in memory can grow unbounded. Cap the in-memory tail at a sensible window (last N=500 events) and document it; the SQLite store remains source of truth for replay.

Snapshot stream resource leak: if the generator returns without closing the bus subscription, listeners pile up. Mitigation: try/finally around the subscription and an integration test that opens/closes 100 streams and asserts bus listener count returns to zero.
acceptance: burrow watch in a workspace with ≥1 active burrow renders a multi-panel TUI that updates live as agents emit events, with header / burrow list / detail pane / keybind footer.

burrow watch --json emits NDJSON DashboardSnapshot envelopes to stdout — the exact shape burrow serve will eventually WebSocket-stream — and is consumable by a scripted reader (assert via piped test).
DashboardSnapshot, BurrowCard, RunSummary types are exported from @os-eco/burrow (src/index.ts) and documented in SPEC §27 with the additive-only versioning rule explicit.
renderSnapshot(snap, viewState, termSize) is pure: golden tests pass for fixed inputs across 80×24, 120×40, and a narrow 60×20 layout.
Keypress reducer handles q (quit), j/k (move selection), enter (focus burrow detail), esc (back to list), PgUp/PgDn (scroll detail tail) — covered by unit tests on simulated key sequences.
Ctrl+C while watching cleanly exits the alt-screen buffer (no leftover ANSI state, cursor restored, terminal usable). Verified by integration test that captures stdout post-exit.
Snapshot stream coalesces a burst of N=100 events within the coalescing window into ≤2 snapshot emissions, and always includes the latest state.
Snapshot stream subscription is leak-free: opening and closing 100 streams against a shared bus leaves zero residual listeners.
burrow events --follow and burrow watch can run against the same database concurrently without one starving the other (verified by parallel integration test).
All quality gates pass: bun test, bun run lint (--error-on-warnings), bun run typecheck. CHANGELOG entry under [Unreleased] → [0.2.0] is added and version bumped via scripts/version-bump.ts.

sections.context: Burrow's Library API and CLI are the only public surfaces today. The warren control plane (separate sibling repo, planned) needs to drive burrow from another process — the home-server / docker-compose deploy puts warren and burrow in the same container, but they are still distinct Bun processes with no IPC. Shelling out per command and parsing per-line stdout is workable for one-shot commands but breaks down for streaming surfaces (events --follow, watch --follow) where we want a long-lived connection with replay-from-seq, clean SIGTERM teardown, and structured backpressure. SPEC §3.2 originally listed an HTTP API as a V2 non-goal; that decision is now reversed because warren is the V2 motivation and the existing Client namespaces (burrows/runs/inbox/events/agent) have stabilized enough that mirroring them over HTTP is a serialization wrapper, not new design. SPEC §26.5 already pre-committed the wire shape for watch streaming.
sections.approach: Add a `burrow serve` subcommand that runs Bun.serve as a thin HTTP layer over the existing Library API. Routes mirror the Client namespaces 1:1 — POST /burrows, GET /burrows/:id, POST /burrows/:id/runs, GET /burrows/:id/events?follow=1, etc. — so the Library remains the source of truth and the HTTP layer cannot drift. Streaming surfaces (events tail, watch snapshot) emit NDJSON over chunked HTTP; the bytes match `burrow events --json` and `burrow watch --json` exactly so a single client lib targets both, and §26 view-model invariants stay intact. Transport: unix socket is primary (zero-port-allocation, filesystem-permission-controlled, the canonical path for warren-in-same-container); localhost TCP is an opt-in flag for cross-container reach. Auth is a single bearer token from BURROW_API_TOKEN env; loopback-only by default; multi-user is an explicit non-goal (SPEC §3.2 'no multi-tenant auth' still holds — single token is the single-user posture). The HTTP-backed Client wrapper mirrors src/lib/client.ts so consumers (warren, future UIs) can swap transports without touching call sites. Streaming connections plumb AbortController the same way logs/events follow commands do (mx-b3423b). Wire shapes are locked via golden tests (mx-1785cc pattern).

alternatives.name: Library-only (status quo) — warren imports burrow as a Bun module
alternatives.rejected_because: Couples warren's process lifecycle to burrow's. Same-image co-deploy means a warren restart kills in-flight burrow runs. HTTP gives independent process boundaries inside one container, which matches the docker-compose model.

alternatives.name: Use a framework (Hono / Elysia / Express)
alternatives.rejected_because: Bun.serve is sufficient for ~20 routes, half of which are streaming. Frameworks add a runtime dep, cache hit risk on Bun upgrades, and a layer of abstraction that doesn't pay for itself at this scope. Same posture as choosing commander over citty (mx-e32456) — match the existing conservative dep stance.

alternatives.name: gRPC over unix socket
alternatives.rejected_because: Every consumer (warren, future UIs, ad-hoc curl scripts, third-party integrations) needs codegen. HTTP+JSON matches the project's shell-out-friendly aesthetic and keeps `curl http://localhost/.../events?follow=1` viable as a debug surface.

alternatives.name: Server-Sent Events (SSE) for streaming
alternatives.rejected_because: NDJSON over chunked HTTP matches the existing CLI --json wire shape byte-for-byte. SSE adds 'event:' / 'data:' framing that consumers would have to strip. The whole point is one wire shape across CLI and HTTP.

alternatives.name: Spec out warren first, then design the API to match
alternatives.rejected_because: Warren's design depends on the API surface, not the other way around. The Client namespaces have already stabilized; the HTTP surface follows them mechanically. Speccing warren first means guessing at the contract and reworking when it lands.

steps.title: Wire envelope + Bun.serve skeleton with routing scaffold
steps.description: Define request/response types in src/server/types.ts. Build the route dispatcher (Bun.serve handler) that maps METHOD + path → namespace handler. All handlers throw NotImplementedError. Wire shapes are JSON; errors use a stable { error: { code, message, hint? } } envelope mirroring src/core/errors.ts mappings. No auth, no streaming yet — just routing.

steps.title: CRUD handlers for burrows/runs/inbox/agents namespaces
steps.description: Implement the non-streaming routes by delegating to the existing Client namespaces (src/lib/client.ts). Burrows: create/list/get/stop/destroy/fork. Runs: create/list/get/cancel. Inbox: send/list/markRead. Agents: list/installCheck. Each handler: validate input via Zod schema, call Client method, serialize response. Reuse error mappings from src/cli/main.ts exitCodeFor → HTTP status (404 for NotFound, 400 for Validation, 500 for runtime/sandbox). No new business logic — pure wrapping.

steps.title: Streaming handlers: events tail + watch snapshot (NDJSON over chunked HTTP)
steps.description: GET /burrows/:id/events?follow=1&since=<seq> and GET /events?follow=1 — subscribe via EventBus, emit NDJSON lines matching the §14.1 envelope. Replay-from-seq reads EventsRepo.listByBurrow with seq filter before switching to live tail. GET /watch?follow=1 mirrors streamSnapshots() per §26.5, emitting DashboardSnapshot lines. Connection lifecycle: AbortController on req.signal, finally-block cleanup mirroring src/cli/commands/logs.ts (mx-b3423b convention). Backpressure via TransformStream — drop oldest events on slow consumer with a structured 'lagged' event before disconnect.

steps.title: Auth (bearer token) + transport plumbing (unix socket primary, localhost TCP opt-in)
steps.description: Bearer auth middleware reads BURROW_API_TOKEN from env. Missing/invalid token → 401. Loopback bypass via --no-auth flag for local-only development. Transport: --socket <path> binds Bun.serve to a unix socket (default: $XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/burrow.sock or ~/.local/share/burrow/sock); --port <n> binds 127.0.0.1:n. At least one transport must be specified; both can be on simultaneously. AuthProvider interface seam (default = bearer impl) so future multi-user can land additively without rewriting routes.

steps.title: `burrow serve` CLI command + graceful shutdown
steps.description: New subcommand in src/cli/commands/serve.ts wired into src/cli/main.ts. Flags: --socket <path>, --port <n>, --no-auth (loopback only). On startup: bind transport(s), print bound addresses, register SIGINT/SIGTERM handlers that abort in-flight requests and close the server cleanly. Exit codes per §16. Help text covers the auth contract clearly. doctor adds a soft check that BURROW_API_TOKEN is set if --no-auth is not used.

steps.title: HTTP-backed Client mirror (src/lib/http-client.ts)
steps.description: New module exporting an HTTP-backed Client with the same TypeScript interface as src/lib/client.ts. Constructor takes { baseUrl | socketPath, token }. Methods serialize args to HTTP requests, deserialize responses, surface server-side errors as the same Error subclasses (NotFoundError, ValidationError, etc.) the local Client throws — so callers can swap transports by changing one line. Streaming methods return AsyncIterable<T> backed by fetch's ReadableStream + line splitter. Warren and future UIs import this; the in-process Client stays for CLI use.

steps.title: Integration test suite end-to-end against `burrow serve`
steps.description: tests/server/*.test.ts: spawn `burrow serve` on an ephemeral unix socket, exercise every namespace via the HTTP-backed Client, verify the full create-burrow → run → events-tail → destroy loop. Streaming tests: assert that NDJSON bytes from /events?follow match `burrow events --follow --json` line-for-line for the same input. Auth tests: 401 on missing/invalid token. Lock tests: golden wire-shape fixtures per route (mx-1785cc pattern) so any breaking change must be deliberate.

steps.title: README + CHANGELOG + SPEC.md §27 cross-link
steps.description: Add SPEC.md §27 'HTTP API (`burrow serve`)' that points at this plan id (pl-xxx) and seed (burrow-1d64) for the design record, with a brief one-paragraph orientation in the spec itself. README gets a 'Programmatic access' section with the unix-socket example. CHANGELOG entry under Unreleased. Bump version per the existing version-bump convention (mx-16ca36) — minor bump (new feature, additive). Update SPEC §3.2 to reflect that HTTP is no longer a non-goal.

risks: Long-lived NDJSON connections need careful AbortController plumbing or `burrow serve` SIGTERM hangs on disconnect. Mitigation: reuse the convention from logs/events follow (mx-b3423b) — req.signal → AbortController → finally cleanup.

Wire shape divergence between CLI --json output and HTTP responses would force consumers to handle two shapes. Mitigation: extract shared serializers used by both surfaces; lock-test fixtures per route (mx-1785cc pattern).

Auth model creep — single bearer token may not survive a future multi-user desire. Mitigation: AuthProvider interface seam from day one so additive landing is possible without rewriting handlers. Single-user is still the explicit V1 posture.

Bwrap-in-container nesting is empirically validated for the agent-in-burrow case (mx-94901b) but `burrow serve` running inside a container that itself spawns burrows produces a third level of nesting we haven't exercised. Mitigation: add an integration test that runs `burrow serve` inside the .devcontainer image and spawns a burrow through the API — should Just Work given mx-94901b's flag set, but verify.

Streaming backpressure on slow consumers could OOM the server if subscriber queues grow unbounded. Mitigation: bounded queue per subscriber, drop-oldest with a structured 'lagged' event then close, same posture as the existing in-memory EventBus.
BURROW_API_TOKEN handling: don't accidentally log it. Mitigation: pino redact config, and explicit test that it doesn't appear in any log output.
acceptance: `burrow serve --socket /tmp/burrow.sock` starts, binds the socket, and accepts requests; SIGINT closes cleanly within 1s.
Every method on src/lib/client.ts has an HTTP equivalent that returns the same shape (golden-test verified per route).
NDJSON output of `curl --unix-socket ... /events?follow=1` matches `burrow events --follow --json` byte-for-byte over the same input.
Replay-from-seq: `GET /burrows/:id/events?since=<seq>&follow=1` emits all events with seq>since in order before switching to live tail, with no duplicates and no gaps.
Bearer auth: missing or invalid BURROW_API_TOKEN returns 401; valid token passes; --no-auth flag bypasses for loopback-only use.
HTTP-backed Client (src/lib/http-client.ts) completes a full create-burrow → prompt → events-tail → destroy loop against `burrow serve` with the same code shape as the in-process Client.
Integration test runs `burrow serve` inside the .devcontainer (mx-94901b/mx-c085ba flag set) and spawns a real burrow through the API end-to-end.
bun test && bun run lint && bun run typecheck pass cleanly.
SPEC.md §27 lands with a one-line pointer to this plan and seed; SPEC §3.2 updated to remove the HTTP non-goal.

sections.context: Warren's run-spawn flow seeds burrow workspaces by writing directly to burrow.workspacePath from disk (warren/src/runs/seed.ts) — three drops: .canopy/agent.json, .mulch/expertise/*.jsonl, .seeds/workflow.txt. This works today only because warren and burrow are co-tenanted in one container and share /data. The moment burrow runs on a remote host (R-02), those writes break — there is no shared filesystem. Even co-tenanted, this path violates the warren↔burrow seam: every other interaction is HTTP with a typed contract; this one reaches past the API onto disk. Warren's SPEC §11.A literally waves at it ("invokes ml record inside the burrow workspace via burrow exec or equivalent"); the equivalent doesn't exist. R-07 closes that gap.
sections.approach: Add a workspace-mutation HTTP surface to burrow with two complementary shapes. (1) Provision-time seed payload on POST /burrows accepting an optional seed: { files: [{ path, mode?, contents }, ...] } — files written into the new workspace before the burrow is returned, atomic with provisioning, single round-trip for warren's batch case. (2) Standalone POST /burrows/:id/files with the same envelope for post-provision writes and any future top-up cases. Plus GET /burrows/:id/files?path=… for warren's reap step (mulch records read back at run end). All writes constrained within workspacePath via a shared path-validation primitive: no symlink escape, no overwrite of .git/ or sandbox-owned paths. Warren consumes via @os-eco/burrow HttpClient and deletes the disk-writing code in src/runs/seed.ts.

alternatives.name: Shared filesystem between warren and burrow
alternatives.rejected_because: Breaks isolation contract, infeasible across hosts (R-02 puts burrow on a different machine), and inherits the same seam-violation smell — warren still reaches past the API contract.

alternatives.name: Generic POST /burrows/:id/exec for arbitrary commands
alternatives.rejected_because: Strictly more powerful but vastly larger attack surface and harder to validate. Workspace mutation is the actual need; arbitrary exec is a future item if a real use case emerges.

alternatives.name: Self-seeding agents (agent definitions include seeding logic)
alternatives.rejected_because: Pushes persistence mechanics into every agent definition, couples agents to storage details, and breaks the canopy/mulch separation. Reap step would still need the read API anyway.

steps.title: OpenAPI schemas + golden test
steps.description: Add `seed` field to POST /burrows request schema; define POST /burrows/:id/files and GET /burrows/:id/files routes in src/server/openapi/spec.ts. Update __golden__ snapshot. Locks the wire shape before handlers exist.

steps.title: Path validation primitive
steps.description: Pure function in src/server/ (or src/core/ if it doesn't touch core types) that rejects: paths outside workspacePath after realpath resolution, symlink-escape attempts, overwrites of .git/, .burrow/, and other sandbox-owned paths. Unit tests with adversarial inputs: relative paths, .., absolute paths, symlinks pointing outside.

steps.title: Server handlers for seed + files
steps.description: Implement POST /burrows seed payload processing (write files into the new workspace before returning 201), POST /burrows/:id/files (post-provision write), and GET /burrows/:id/files?path=… (read for reap). All use the path-validation primitive. Integration tests cover the full warren seed+reap roundtrip against a real burrow. Quota: 10 MB total per call, 1 MB per file (defaults, configurable).

steps.title: HttpClient methods
steps.description: In @os-eco/burrow library, add `burrows.create({ ..., seed })` accepting the seed payload, `burrows.files.write(burrowId, files)`, and `burrows.files.read(burrowId, path)`. Typed mirror of server. Update HttpClient golden + types so warren can adopt without hand-rolled HTTP.

risks: Path-validation bugs let writes escape workspacePath. Mitigation: realpath before check, deny-list of internal paths, fuzz tests with symlink-escape inputs.

OpenAPI spec drift between hand-authored spec.ts and handler implementation. Mitigation: golden test (already the convention per mulch pattern mx-f5d9c8) plus type-checking handlers against generated types.
Binary-content callers eventually want multipart instead of JSON+base64. Mitigation: ship JSON+base64 V1 since the only known consumer is text; document multipart as a future-compatible extension.
Warren's switchover lags burrow's release, leaving warren still doing disk writes. Mitigation: file the warren-side switchover as a downstream seed in warren's tracker, gate on R-07 release.
acceptance: POST /burrows accepts an optional seed payload and writes the listed files into the new workspace atomically before returning 201.
POST /burrows/:id/files writes files into the burrow's workspace and rejects paths outside workspacePath, symlink escapes, and overwrites of .git/ or sandbox-owned paths.
GET /burrows/:id/files?path=… returns file contents from within workspacePath under the same constraints; rejects the same escape attempts.
HttpClient exposes typed methods for create-with-seed, files.write, and files.read.
OpenAPI golden test covers all three new shapes; spec.test.ts passes.
Integration test demonstrates warren's seed+reap loop end-to-end via HttpClient against a real burrow, with no disk writes by the consumer.

sections.context: V1 burrow ships only LocalProvider, leaving SPEC §23's last success criterion — "a future FlyProvider can be added without modifying any file under src/core/, src/db/, src/runtime/, src/inbox/, src/events/, or src/runner/" — unverified. Until at least one remote provider lands, the BurrowProvider seam claim is unproven; until two land, "the seam is generic" is just "the seam is Fly-shaped." Warren's deploy story (warren SPEC §10.2) requires a remote-daemon model: one long-lived burrow serve on a Fly Machine, many burrows dispatched into it. R-01 (DEPLOY.md) already established that Fly Machines map to on-host posture (no four-flag waiver), so the substrate is ready; R-02 is what actually exercises it.
sections.approach: Build FlyProvider and SshProvider in tandem under interpretation A (remote daemon, not per-burrow Fly Machine boot). One Fly Machine or SSH host runs `burrow serve` on an HTTPS endpoint; many burrows are provisioned into that one daemon via POST /burrows. The BurrowProvider seam splits into machine-lifecycle and daemon-binding responsibilities: SshProvider is registration-only (user already deployed per DEPLOY.md, FlyProvider owns lifecycle + binding. The shared daemon-binding code is HttpClient-pointed-at-a-URL with bearer auth from a named profile in ~/.config/burrow/remotes.toml. Fly + SSH ship together because anything Fly-specific that leaks past the lifecycle boundary will fail to compile against SshProvider — that's the seam load-test SPEC §23 calls for. Workspace seeding goes through R-07's HTTP API (hard dependency), not direct-disk writes.

alternatives.name: Ship FlyProvider only; defer SshProvider
alternatives.rejected_because: Fly-only doesn't actually load-test "the seam is generic" — Fly-isms can leak into core and only surface when a future second provider gets blocked. Shipping Fly + SSH together is the real seam test.

alternatives.name: Interpretation B — per-burrow Fly Machine boot
alternatives.rejected_because: Heavy provisioning per `burrow up`, doesn't match warren-on-Fly's deploy story (one persistent machine, many burrows), and 15-60s cold start per burrow. May ship later as a scale-out layer on top of (A); not the same product.

alternatives.name: Reverse-tunnel from caller to remote burrow
alternatives.rejected_because: Adds complexity (tunnel lifecycle, NAT) without solving anything. HTTPS-to-publicly-reachable-daemon is simpler, matches DEPLOY.md's reverse-proxy posture, and reuses existing Bun.serve.

alternatives.name: Multi-tenant a single Fly Machine across warrens
alternatives.rejected_because: Explicitly out of scope. One machine = one warren = many burrows is the model; cross-tenant on a single machine reopens isolation/auth questions the SPEC keeps closed.

steps.title: BurrowProvider seam shape + import-graph guardrail
steps.description: Define the BurrowProvider interface split: machine-lifecycle (provision/teardown the host) vs. daemon-binding (HttpClient pointed at a URL). Refactor LocalProvider to implement both responsibilities (no behavior change). Add a forbidden-imports test that fails if any new file under src/core, src/db, src/runtime, src/inbox, src/events, or src/runner imports provider-implementation symbols. This locks SPEC §23's last criterion as a build-time invariant before any remote provider exists.

steps.title: Daemon-binding code path (RemoteBinding)
steps.description: Extract the shared remote-binding logic: HttpClient over HTTPS with bearer auth, profile resolution from ~/.config/burrow/remotes.toml, error mapping (network errors, auth failures, 5xx), and connection retries. Tested against a local `burrow serve` exposed over HTTPS-on-loopback as if it were remote. RemoteBinding is provider-agnostic — same code consumed by SshProvider and FlyProvider.

steps.title: Remote profile management (`burrow login`, `burrow remote`)
steps.description: CLI commands: `burrow login fly` (records FLY_API_TOKEN + app handle), `burrow login ssh <url> --token <T>` (records named SSH remote), `burrow remote list`, `burrow remote remove <name>`. Storage in ~/.config/burrow/remotes.toml with file mode 0600. Token redaction in logs and `--json` output. `burrow doctor` checks profile reachability.

steps.title: SshProvider (registration-only, no machine lifecycle)
steps.description: Implements BurrowProvider for a user-deployed remote daemon. No machine lifecycle — ssp the user has already deployed `burrow serve` per DEPLOY.md. Consumes RemoteBinding + a profile from `burrow login ssh`. Ships first because the lifecycle-free path is the simpler validation that the seam supports zero-lifecycle providers.

steps.title: FlyProvider (machine lifecycle: attach-only V1)
steps.description: Implements BurrowProvider for a Fly Machine. V1 is attach-only: user runs `fly launch` against a published burrow image; FlyProvider connects to the existing app and ensures a Machine is running (start if stopped, error if missing). Lifecycle methods: ensure_machine_running, get_machine_url. Consumes RemoteBinding for daemon traffic. Provisioning (FlyProvider creates the app on first use) is a future enhancement.

steps.title: CLI integration (`burrow up --remote <name>`)
steps.description: Wire `--remote <name>` into `burrow up`, `burrow events`, `burrow ship`, and other burrow CLI commands. Resolves the named profile, picks the matching provider (Fly | SSH), dispatches over HTTPS. All commands work transparently against local, Fly-remote, and SSH-remote.

steps.title: Conformance suite + DEPLOY.md update
steps.description: Single test set runs against LocalProvider, FlyProvider (against a real Fly app in CI), and SshProvider (against a containerized burrow serve standing in for an SSH host). Validates seam-uniformity at the test layer. DEPLOY.md gets a new section: deploying burrow as a remote daemon, the `burrow login` flow, and warren-on-Fly's recommended setup. README updates for `--remote`.

risks: SPEC §23 seam violation surfaces during implementation (a forbidden file actually needs to change to support remote). Mitigation: import-graph guardrail in step 0 fails fast; if the seam genuinely needs to evolve, it's a SPEC change, not a quiet edit.

Cold-start UX latency on Fly Machine boot for laptop users running `burrow up --remote fly`. Mitigation: warren-on-Fly model amortizes (one boot per session, not per burrow); document the tradeoff for laptop callers; warm pool is a future scale enhancement.

Credential leak: Fly secrets, SSH tokens, BURROW_API_TOKEN in logs or `--json` output. Mitigation: explicit redaction in profile-load code path, redaction tests against pino + JSON serializer, secret values never reach the event log.

Conformance suite false-pass — all three providers passing the same suite doesn't prove the seam is generic if the suite tests only LocalProvider-shaped behavior. Mitigation: include cross-provider parity tests (same `burrow up` produces same workspace shape, same event stream, same lifecycle).

Workspace seeding still on disk during R-02 work because warren switchover lags. Mitigation: R-07 ships first (hard dependency); switch warren before R-02 hits production via a coordinated release on warren's side.
acceptance: BurrowProvider seam supports LocalProvider plus at least one remote provider with no changes under src/core/, src/db/, src/runtime/, src/inbox/, src/events/, or src/runner/ — verified by an import-graph test.

`burrow login ssh https://host --token T` records a named profile; `burrow up --remote <name>` provisions a burrow on that host over HTTPS and returns a Burrow record byte-for-byte equivalent to LocalProvider.

`burrow login fly` records Fly credentials; `burrow up --remote fly` ensures the Fly Machine is running and provisions a burrow on it; `burrow up` against a stopped machine starts it, against a missing app errors clearly.
All burrow CLI commands (up, events, ship, watch, shell, runs, ...) work transparently against local, Fly-remote, and SSH-remote profiles.
Conformance test suite passes against LocalProvider, FlyProvider, and SshProvider with cross-provider parity assertions on workspace shape, event stream, and lifecycle.
Tokens and credentials never appear in logs, --json output, or error messages — verified by redaction tests.
DEPLOY.md updated with remote-daemon deployment recipe, `burrow login` flow, and warren-on-Fly setup; README documents `--remote`.

outcomeNote: Not pursued. Plan's stated motivation (warren-on-Fly requires remote-daemon model) contradicts warren SPEC §10.2 + §3.2 — warren and burrow are co-located in one container over a unix socket. SPEC §23 seam-validation stands alone but lacks a concrete consumer. Deferred to ROADMAP R-02 [deferred] until laptop `burrow up --remote`, warren V2 worker pool, or greenhouse remote dispatch actually pulls on the seam.

sections.context: Today warren and `burrow serve` are sibling processes inside one container, talking over a unix socket at `/var/run/burrow.sock` (warren SPEC §5.1, §5.2, §10.3). Warren is being positioned for self-hosting at 50+ engineer orgs (seed c47a), which makes single-host the wrong shape. To enable cross-host dispatch, burrow must (a) bind safely to a non-loopback interface with a documented auth + TLS posture, (b) expose a drain endpoint so warren can quiesce a worker for rolling restarts, and (c) prove the HTTP-create → dispatcher path is rock-solid before warren leans on it cross-host (the fix shipped at 5def5e9 for burrow-7b97 but has never been exercised cross-process from a subprocess test). This plan covers the burrow-side surface only — non-loopback bind safety, `POST /admin/drain`, OpenAPI + DEPLOY.md documentation, and ROADMAP supersession of R-02. Warren-side wiring (worker pool, placement, fan-out, `[workers]` config, SPEC update, two-worker integration test) is tracked separately in the warren repo as the counterpart plan; this plan's release step coordinates the joint version bump.
sections.approach: Burrow stays stateful per-worker (SQLite + sandboxes per host). The CLI gains a `--bind-host` flag (default stays `127.0.0.1`, so today's deploys don't regress — see resolveTransport in src/cli/commands/serve.ts:142-170 and the loopback-only default in src/server/server.ts:35); when `--bind-host` resolves to anything non-loopback AND `--no-auth` is set, startup is refused with a clear error directing the operator at `BURROW_API_TOKEN`. Transport is plain HTTP behind operator-supplied TLS termination at a reverse proxy on each worker (VPC-private threat model: Tailscale / AWS VPC / Fly private network); auth is the existing bearer-token path (src/server/auth.ts:32-126). mTLS is an explicit future R-NN, not V1. Exactly one new admin route, `POST /admin/drain`, lands on the agent listener (no separate admin port — the bearer-auth boundary is sufficient under the VPC threat model). Drain state lives on the dispatcher (src/runner/dispatcher.ts): when set, both `POST /burrows` and `POST /burrows/:id/runs` return 503 `{error:{code:'worker_draining'}}`; in-flight runs and streams continue to terminal state; graceful shutdown blocks until both sets drain. OpenAPI scaffolding (SPEC §27, burrow-d3ea) absorbs the new route. ROADMAP R-02 (FlyProvider + SshProvider, deferred 2026-05-09) is marked `[superseded by burrow-c47a]` since this work delivers the same operational goal (multi-host concurrency) via a simpler contract (config + pool, no provider plugins). Phase 0 of the plan locks the cross-process dispatch contract with a real subprocess e2e test so warren has a reliable contract to depend on. **Warren-side wiring is tracked separately in burrow-62ce's warren-side counterpart parent seed and plan — see the release step (the only step in this plan that spans both repos).**

alternatives.name: Per-burrow Fly Machine via FlyProvider (R-02 Interpretation B)
alternatives.rejected_because: Cold-start cost per `burrow up` is multiple seconds even on Fly; warren's UX assumes sub-second provisioning. R-02 itself rejected this in favor of Interpretation A (long-lived daemon per machine) — this plan keeps Interpretation A but ships it as static config rather than a generic `BurrowProvider` seam exercise. No `FlyProvider` / `SshProvider` code in V1; if Fly auto-provisioning is ever wanted it lands as a worker-registration helper on the warren side, not a burrow-side provider.

alternatives.name: Lift burrow's SQLite up to warren — workers become stateless
alternatives.rejected_because: Burrow's SQLite carries run history, the events stream, sandbox metadata, and crash-recovery state (src/server/server.ts:97-110 dispatcher recovery sweep, runs the queued→failed reset before serving any request). Promoting that to warren means rewriting burrow's storage layer and forfeiting per-worker crash recovery semantics. Burrow already ships durable per-worker state; the cheaper move is to teach warren which worker owns which burrow.

alternatives.name: Separate admin listener / port for `/admin/drain`
alternatives.rejected_because: Under the VPC-private threat model the bearer-auth boundary is the meaningful security perimeter, not the TCP port. A separate admin port doubles the listener surface and the operator-facing config (now two ports per worker to expose / firewall) for zero security gain. Adding the route to the existing agent listener keeps the V1 operational surface minimal.

alternatives.name: mTLS between warren and workers in V1
alternatives.rejected_because: Threat model is VPC-private (Tailscale / AWS VPC / Fly private network), so bearer-over-TLS at a reverse proxy is sufficient. mTLS adds cert provisioning + rotation + a CA story burrow V1 has no machinery for. Tracked as a future R-NN; not in this plan.

alternatives.name: Spec out R-02's `BurrowProvider` seam in burrow first
alternatives.rejected_because: R-02 was deferred because nothing pulled on the seam. This work is the pull. Building the seam on speculation duplicates work — a static-config worker pool on the warren side is a simpler V1 with the same operational surface (multi-host concurrency); if a future use case (`burrow up --remote my-vps` from a laptop) wants the `BurrowProvider` model, it lands additively on top of this rather than blocking c47a on a generic plugin system.

steps.title: [burrow] Verify and document `burrow serve` executor: cross-process e2e + DEPLOY.md note
steps.description: Phase 0 — Unblock. The run executor wiring shipped in commit 5def5e9 (burrow-7b97): `startRunDispatcher` is started before `startServer` in src/cli/commands/serve.ts:97-110, with crash-recovery + create-time hook. Before warren routes any cross-host traffic, add an e2e test in burrow that spawns `burrow serve` as a subprocess (TCP transport), POSTs `/burrows` + `/burrows/:id/runs`, and asserts the run reaches `succeeded` (today's serve.test.ts covers in-process). Adds a one-paragraph DEPLOY.md note that `burrow serve` is now a complete agent host (dispatcher + HTTP) rather than enqueue-only — this is the contract warren depends on for remote workers. No code changes in src/runner/dispatcher.ts beyond the test; the goal is to lock the contract before warren leans on it. Cross-repo: this step blocks the warren-side `workers` table step (warren parent / warren plan, step 0) — the warren-side plan documents that inbound dependency in prose.

steps.title: [burrow] `serve --bind-host` flag with localhost default; reject non-loopback when auth is disabled
steps.description: Today `resolveTransport` in src/cli/commands/serve.ts:142-170 reads `--host` and `--port` and defaults to `127.0.0.1`. Promote `--host` to a primary surface: rename internal docs to `--bind-host`, keep `--host` as alias for back-compat, document the loopback-only default in `emitStartupBanner`. Add a startup-time check: if `--bind-host` resolves to anything other than `127.0.0.1` / `::1` / `localhost` AND `--no-auth` is set, refuse to start with a clear error directing the operator to set `BURROW_API_TOKEN`. This is the only safety rail in V1 — TLS termination is delegated to the operator's reverse proxy. The default behavior (no flag, unix socket) is unchanged.

steps.title: [burrow] DEPLOY.md: multi-worker topology + TLS-at-reverse-proxy recipe
steps.description: Document the V1 operational posture in DEPLOY.md (or a new docs/multi-worker.md cross-linked from DEPLOY.md). Cover: (a) each worker runs `burrow serve --bind-host 0.0.0.0 --port 7777` behind nginx / Caddy / Fly's edge with TLS termination, (b) `BURROW_API_TOKEN` is shared across the pool (single token; per-worker token rotation tracked as future hardening), (c) the VPC-private threat model (Tailscale / AWS VPC / Fly private network) and what is NOT defended (public-internet exposure), (d) a sample nginx config and a Caddyfile, (e) explicit note that mTLS is a future capability (R-NN), not V1. This is documentation only — no code — but it's the contract warren's worker-pool config will reference.

steps.title: [burrow] `POST /admin/drain`: gate new run + burrow creation while existing work finishes
steps.description: Adds one new route to ROUTE_TABLE (src/server/routes.ts:83-110): `POST /admin/drain` with body `{drain: true|false}` on the same listener as the agent API (no separate admin port — VPC-private threat model means the bearer-auth boundary is sufficient). Drain state lives on the dispatcher (src/runner/dispatcher.ts) — when set, BOTH `POST /burrows` (BurrowsClient.create) AND `POST /burrows/:id/runs` (RunsClient.create) return 503 `{error:{code:'worker_draining'}}`. Drain semantics for in-flight work: in-flight runs continue to completion; in-flight `GET /runs/:id/stream` and `/burrows/:id/events` consumers keep streaming until they close on their own; the worker's graceful shutdown blocks until both the run set and the stream set drain to empty. Why a dedicated route rather than SIGTERM: rolling restarts in a multi-worker pool need finer control than process-level signals (warren needs to know whether the worker accepted the drain). Auth-required (not `/healthz`-style exempt — only the warren that registered the worker should be able to drain it). One unit test + one e2e in serve.test.ts asserting (a) new POSTs are refused, (b) in-flight runs finish, (c) in-flight streams remain open until client close, (d) graceful shutdown blocks on both sets. Cross-repo: this step blocks the warren-side `Wire BurrowClientPool into spawnRun dispatch flow` step (warren plan, step 3) — the warren-side plan documents that inbound dependency in prose.

steps.title: [burrow] OpenAPI doc update: `/admin/drain` + bind-host posture note
steps.description: Extend `src/server/openapi/spec.ts` with the `/admin/drain` route schema, regenerate the golden at `src/server/openapi/__golden__/openapi.json`, update `spec.test.ts`. Add a top-level `info.description` paragraph that documents the bind-host / TLS-at-proxy posture so external consumers reading the spec know what auth + transport guarantees they get. Mechanical; rides on the existing OpenAPI scaffolding (SPEC §27, burrow-d3ea).

steps.title: [burrow] ROADMAP.md: mark R-02 superseded by c47a; SPEC §27 cross-link to multi-worker doc
steps.description: Edit ROADMAP.md lines 98-192: change R-02 status from `[deferred]` to `[superseded by burrow-c47a]` with a one-paragraph note explaining that c47a delivers the operational goal (cloud-deployed burrow swarms, cross-host warren dispatch) via a simpler contract (static config + per-worker daemon on the warren side) rather than the `BurrowProvider` plugin model. SPEC §27 (line 1300) gets one sentence pointing at the multi-worker doc from step 2 and noting `burrow serve` now supports non-loopback binding behind operator-supplied TLS. No file changes under src/.

steps.title: [burrow + warren] Release notes + CHANGELOG entries + minor-version bump on both
steps.description: Burrow: CHANGELOG entry under Unreleased noting `--bind-host`, `POST /admin/drain`, the R-02 supersession, and the multi-worker doc. Minor bump via `bun run version:bump minor`. Warren: CHANGELOG entry noting the worker pool, `[workers]` config, the §3.2 non-goal removal, and the new admin endpoints (per the warren-side plan). Minor bump. Both bumps are additive — zero-config deploys are unchanged on both sides. Final `bun test && bun run lint && bun run typecheck` clean on both repos before tagging. This step is the only cross-repo step in this plan; it depends on every preceding burrow step here AND on the full warren-side plan completing. The warren-side plan's final integration-test step (warren plan, step 9) is the warren-side gate for this release.

risks: Single shared `BURROW_API_TOKEN` across the worker pool: compromise of any worker leaks the token for the whole pool. Mitigation: VPC-private threat model — the bearer is gating a network boundary that's already locked down (Tailscale / AWS VPC / Fly private network); compromise implies network-layer breach, which is the actual threat boundary. Rotation is one env-var update across N workers + warren (vs. N independent rotations); document the runbook in DEPLOY.md (step 2). Per-worker tokens tracked as a future hardening R-NN.

Non-loopback bind with `--no-auth` is footgun-shaped. Mitigation: step 1 makes `burrow serve` refuse to start in that combination. The operator has to consciously set `BURROW_API_TOKEN` to expose burrow over TCP.

Dispatcher crash on a remote worker leaves runs queued forever (regression of burrow-7b97). Mitigation: step 0 locks the cross-process dispatch contract with a real subprocess test; warren's per-worker probe (warren-side plan) surfaces a dead dispatcher within 30s.

TLS termination is the operator's job (V1). Misconfigured proxy could expose burrow plaintext on the VPC. Mitigation: DEPLOY.md (step 2) explicit nginx/Caddy recipes; warren probe (warren-side plan) uses HTTPS URLs only so a plaintext leak is observable from warren-side audit logs.

Drain semantics under load: long-tail streams could indefinitely block graceful shutdown. Mitigation: step 3's e2e asserts the in-flight set drains; if production reveals a stuck-stream pathology, follow-up adds a configurable drain-timeout with forced close (tracked as R-NN if/when observed).
acceptance: Zero-config back-compat: `burrow serve` with no `--bind-host` flag continues to bind to `127.0.0.1` / unix socket exactly as today (verified by existing serve.test.ts passing unmodified).
`burrow serve --bind-host 0.0.0.0 --port 7777` with `BURROW_API_TOKEN` set accepts TCP requests from outside loopback and authenticates them via bearer.
`burrow serve --bind-host 0.0.0.0 --no-auth` exits non-zero with a clear error pointing at `BURROW_API_TOKEN`.

`POST /admin/drain {drain: true}` returns 200; subsequent `POST /burrows/:id/runs` returns 503 `{error:{code:'worker_draining'}}`; existing in-flight runs continue to terminal state; `POST /admin/drain {drain: false}` restores acceptance.
burrow ROADMAP R-02 status is `[superseded by burrow-c47a]`.
Burrow cross-process e2e from step 0 confirms `burrow serve` over TCP drives a queued run to `succeeded` (locks burrow-7b97's contract for cross-host use).
OpenAPI golden includes `/admin/drain` and the bind-host posture note in `info.description`.
`bun test && bun run lint && bun run typecheck` clean on burrow.

No `BurrowProvider` plugin code lands in burrow (the c47a contract is config + pool on the warren side, not a new seam in burrow). mTLS is mentioned as a future R-NN in burrow ROADMAP, not implemented.
Release step (step 6) gates on the warren-side plan reaching its acceptance — both repos bump minor and ship together.

sections.context: Burrow ships three built-in runtimes (claude-code, sapling, codex) wrapping the major non-interactive coding agents. Pi (@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent v0.74.0) is a fourth viable headless agent — its `--mode rpc` exposes a rich JSONL command/event protocol that fits burrow's spawn-per-turn dispatch model. The Greenhouse → Overstory → burrow pipeline already lets each burrow.toml `[[agents]]` row pick a runtime, so adding pi widens dispatch choices without forcing users in. This seed scoped a parity-baseline integration; the plan below grounds that scope after an external read of pi's RPC docs (event vocabulary, framing rules, exit semantics) and an internal walk of burrow's runtime contract (src/runtime/runtime.ts, registry.ts, dispatch.ts) — see commit-attached notes for the captured trace.
sections.approach: Add a built-in `piRuntime` in src/runtime/pi.ts following the existing AgentRuntime contract (claude-code/sapling/codex pattern). Spawn command is `pi --mode rpc --no-session --no-extensions --provider anthropic --model <pinned>`; each run writes one `{"type":"prompt","message":"<prompt + steering prefix>"}` line to stdin then closes it, pi drains, streams events on stdout, and exits — same flow shape as claude-code's stream-json mode, so src/runner/dispatch.ts needs no changes. A new parser src/runtime/parsers/pi.ts maps pi's wider event vocabulary (agent_start/end, turn_start/end, message_start/update/end, tool_execution_start/update/end, queue_update, compaction_*, auto_retry_*, extension_error, response) into burrow's existing RunEvent.kind taxonomy (text/thinking/tool_use/tool_result/state_change/telemetry/stderr) — lossy collapse with the original envelope preserved in payload. SPEC §14.1 already declares kinds additive and RunEvent.kind is a bare `string` (src/core/types.ts:32), so future widening (e.g. promoting compaction to a first-class kind) is non-breaking. Forces `--provider anthropic` so users don't get a Gemini surprise (pi's CLI default is Gemini per upstream docs). Forces `--no-extensions` so the extension_ui_request RPC dialog protocol never fires — burrow's dispatcher has no path to respond to mid-stream UI requests, and ignoring them would hang an extension waiting on user input. `supportsResume:false` for V1 (paired with `--no-session`); resume support is a follow-up seed once we observe pi session-id propagation in the stream. Steering messages render as `[STEERING] (priority: P) <body>` prefix lines (parity with sapling/codex's composeXPrompt helpers). envPassthrough mirrors the claude-code minimal contract — ANTHROPIC_API_KEY, ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN, ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL — so the sandbox forwards only what the pinned provider needs. installCheck goes through the shared runVersionCheck helper, populating InstallCheckResult.path so `burrow up` folds the resolved binary location into SandboxProfile.toolchainPaths (mx-bd1445). Devcontainer image bakes pi at the same pinned version after the claude-code install (mx-20f3b1's image-contract pattern). Tests follow src/runtime/codex.test.ts shape and extend the fakeRuntime pattern in src/runner/dispatcher.test.ts.

alternatives.name: Declarative AgentConfig instead of a built-in runtime
alternatives.rejected_because: src/runtime/declarative.ts's loadAgentConfig handles prompt-via-arg/stdin/file and three parse formats, but pi's RPC stdin contract (`{type:"prompt",message:"..."}` framed JSON over stdin, not raw text) doesn't fit any existing promptDelivery enum. Adding a fourth delivery mode just for pi defeats the point of the declarative seam (it's meant to onboard external CLIs without runtime code). Built-in runtime gives us typed access to pi-specific event mapping and keeps the declarative path simple.

alternatives.name: Widen RunEvent.kind union with pi-specific kinds in SPEC §14.1 up-front
alternatives.rejected_because: We'd be guessing at which pi event types (compaction, auto_retry, queue_update) deserve first-class promotion before any production observation. The kind field is a bare `string` in src/core/types.ts and SPEC §14.1 explicitly says additions are additive — collapsing to state_change with the full envelope preserved in payload keeps the door open for principled promotion later. Premature widening commits SPEC to a vocabulary we may regret.

alternatives.name: Mid-run steering via pi's RPC `steer`/`follow_up` commands
alternatives.rejected_because: Burrow's dispatch.ts (src/runner/dispatch.ts:114-148) writes the prompt blob then closes stdin — same as claude-code. Mid-stream stdin writes would require an inbox-pull loop and per-runtime async stdin writer, which is a cross-cutting overhaul (sapling and a future runtime would benefit too). Track separately as `mid-run steering` follow-up; prepend-on-prompt is the V1 parity baseline.

alternatives.name: supportsResume:true with --session-dir <workspace>/.pi/sessions
alternatives.rejected_because: Pi's session-id surfaces in the `agent_end` and `get_state` payloads, but populating Run.metadataJson.session_id requires the run-loop layer to recognize a new shape and we'd be adding a code path that no caller uses yet. Resume parity with claude-code lands cleanly as a follow-up seed once we've observed actual pi sessions; V1 starts with --no-session (matches codex's one-shot posture).

alternatives.name: Long-lived pi daemon multiplexing multiple runs
alternatives.rejected_because: Burrow's run lifecycle is per-process (one spawn per Run row, sandbox profile rebound each time). A daemon model conflicts with the sandbox lifecycle and the per-run RunEvent.run_id partitioning, and gains nothing because spawn startup is dominated by API latency, not pi boot.

alternatives.name: Forward OPENAI_API_KEY, GEMINI_API_KEY, etc. by default for users who override --provider
alternatives.rejected_because: Forwarding non-Anthropic keys into the sandbox leaks user secrets into a runtime that's pinned to --provider anthropic. Users who want to switch providers can declare additional names in burrow.toml [env] passthrough — that's the per-project escape hatch (mx-d46d5d). V1 envPassthrough stays minimal and aligned with the forced provider.

steps.title: Capture a real pi v0.74.0 RPC stdout trace as a fixture (research validator)
steps.description: Write a throwaway Bun script that spawns `pi --mode rpc --no-session --no-extensions --provider anthropic --model claude-sonnet-4-20250514`, sends one prompt (e.g. `{"type":"prompt","message":"echo OK"}\n`), captures stdout to a JSONL file, and exits cleanly on `agent_end`. Land the captured trace as a test fixture under src/runtime/parsers/__fixtures__/pi-trace.jsonl. Validates upstream docs against actual binary behavior (event ordering, agent_end exit semantics, --no-extensions actually gates extension_ui_request, exit code) before any production code lands. Documents the exact event sequence the parser will see in Step 2.

steps.title: Build src/runtime/parsers/pi.ts + unit tests
steps.description: New parser parsePiEvents(line) returning RuntimeEvent[]. Mapping rules: agent_start/end, turn_start/end, queue_update, compaction_start/end, auto_retry_start/end → state_change on system stream (payload = raw envelope). message_end → emit one text/thinking event per assistant-message content block (parallels parseJsonlClaude's mapAssistantBlock pattern at src/runtime/parsers/jsonl-claude.ts:102-126). message_update deltas dropped for V1 — we surface final text per message, not streaming deltas, matching claude-code's per-content-block grain. tool_execution_start → tool_use; tool_execution_end → tool_result; tool_execution_update dropped. extension_error → error on stderr stream. `response` envelopes (RPC command echoes) → telemetry on system stream. Unknown types → text with raw envelope (same defensive posture as parseStreamJson). Unit tests at src/runtime/parsers/pi.test.ts cover each envelope shape, an invalid-JSON line (graceful text fallback), an empty line (drop), and a U+2028-inside-JSON-string line (locks the LF-only framing invariant; pi docs flag this explicitly).

steps.title: Build src/runtime/pi.ts (piRuntime) + unit tests
steps.description:
Export `piRuntime: AgentRuntime` with id="pi", displayName="Pi", supportsResume=false. buildSpawnCommand returns argv `["pi","--mode","rpc","--no-session","--no-extensions","--provider","anthropic","--model","claude-sonnet-4-20250514"]` and stdin = single line `JSON.stringify({type:"prompt",message: composePiPrompt(prompt, pendingMessages)}) + "\n"`. parseEvents wraps parsePiEvents from Step 2. encodeInboxMessage renders messages as `[STEERING] (priority:

) ` lines joined by \n inside a single prompt envelope (export composePiPrompt for tests + future inbox-layer reuse, parity with composeSaplingPrompt at src/runtime/sapling.ts:73-77). envPassthrough = readonly tuple ["ANTHROPIC_API_KEY","ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN","ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL"]. installCheck = runVersionCheck("pi", ["--version"], { hint: "install pi: `bun install -g @earendil-works/pi-coding-agent`" }). No credentialPaths and no prepareWorkspace for V1 (API-key auth, no session persistence, no settings file). Tests at src/runtime/pi.test.ts mirror codex.test.ts: argv shape lock (every flag asserted explicitly so `--provider anthropic` and `--no-extensions` can't silently disappear), stdin encoding includes prompt + steering prefix, composePiPrompt with/without pending messages, supportsResume false, encodeInboxMessage shape.

steps.title: Register piRuntime in BUILT_IN_RUNTIMES + extend registry tests
steps.description: src/runtime/registry.ts: one import (`import { piRuntime } from "./pi.ts";`) and one tuple entry appended to BUILT_IN_RUNTIMES (src/runtime/registry.ts:23-27). src/runtime/registry.test.ts: assert AgentRegistry boots with four built-ins, `agents.has("pi")` is true, `agents.require("pi").displayName === "Pi"`, and last-write-wins still works for an overriding AgentConfig with id:"pi".

steps.title: Add end-to-end dispatcher test exercising pi event stream
steps.description: Extend src/runner/dispatcher.test.ts using the existing fakeSpawn + real piRuntime: feed the captured trace from Step 1 as stdoutLines, assert the dispatcher persists the expected RunEvent rows (kinds, streams, payloads), and exit code 0 finalizes the run as `succeeded`. Validates the wiring across registry → dispatch → parser → events repo without shelling out. No changes to src/runner/dispatch.ts — the run loop is runtime-agnostic by design.

steps.title: Install pi in .devcontainer/Dockerfile
steps.description: Add `RUN bun install -g @earendil-works/pi-coding-agent@0.74.0` after the existing `RUN bun install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code` line. Tight version pin because pi is pre-1.0 and the RPC vocabulary can drift on minor bumps. Update mulch record mx-20f3b1 (image-contract reference) with the new contract entry: every binary declared in [[agents]] must be on PATH in the devcontainer.

steps.title: RPC-handshake compatibility test (golden fixture lock)
steps.description: New test src/runtime/pi-handshake.test.ts that imports piRuntime, feeds the captured fixture from Step 1 line-by-line through parsePiEvents, and asserts the full sequence of (kind, stream, payload-summary) tuples matches a checked-in golden file. Acts as a canary: a future pi version that reshapes its event JSONL will fail this test loudly instead of silently producing degraded events. Pattern mirrors the OpenAPI golden-file convention (mx-3349b5).

steps.title: SPEC.md §14.1 footnote + CHANGELOG entry + follow-up seeds
steps.description: SPEC.md §14.1: add a one-line footnote that pi-specific event kinds (compaction_*, auto_retry_*, queue_update) currently collapse to state_change with the original envelope preserved in payload; future amendments may promote them to first-class kinds (additive, consumers ignore unknown kinds). CHANGELOG entry: "feat(runtime): add pi runtime — built-in AgentRegistry entry wrapping @earendil-works/pi-coding-agent@0.74.0 via --mode rpc --no-session --no-extensions; provider pinned to anthropic for V1." File follow-up seeds: (a) pi runtime resume support (--session-dir + agent_end session-id capture); (b) cross-runtime mid-run steering (would benefit pi + sapling); (c) widen RunEvent.kind: promote compaction/retry once observed in production. Close burrow-8aff with a pointer to this plan and the children.

risks: Pi's CLI default provider is Google Gemini (per upstream docs). Omitting `--provider anthropic` silently routes prompts to a different provider and bills against GEMINI_API_KEY. Mitigation: hardcode the flag in buildSpawnCommand and lock the full argv shape with a unit test that fails CI if the flag goes missing — same posture as claude-code's --dangerously-skip-permissions assertion (mx-5b6ed2).

Pi's RPC framing requires strict LF-only JSON line splitting (Node readline is non-compliant because it also splits on U+2028/U+2029, which are valid inside JSON strings). Mitigation: burrow's dispatch.ts readLines (src/runner/dispatch.ts:234-255) already uses TextDecoder + manual indexOf('\n'), so we're compliant by default — but add a parser unit test using a fixture line containing a U+2028 inside a JSON string to lock the invariant for future refactors.

extension_ui_request can hang a run if an extension is auto-discovered (workspace .pi/extensions or user-level ~/.pi). Burrow's dispatch.ts closes stdin after the prompt blob and has no path to write `extension_ui_response`. Mitigation: hardcode `--no-extensions`; argv-shape unit test asserts the flag is present; document in src/runtime/pi.ts header comment.

Pi is pre-1.0 (v0.74.0). A minor bump could reshape RPC event JSONL silently. Mitigation: tight version pin in Dockerfile + golden handshake test from Step 7 + a dedicated follow-up seed for the bump cadence (verify against fixture, regenerate if intentional).

Lossy event mapping (compaction_*, auto_retry_*, queue_update) means downstream consumers (greenhouse/warren UI) won't see distinct kinds for retry vs general state change. Mitigation: payload preserves the full envelope so future kind-widening is non-breaking and the data is still observable via grep on payload.type; flag in SPEC §14.1 footnote.

resolveBinaryPath via Bun.which depends on `pi` being on PATH at `burrow up` time; without it the sandbox can fail the same way claude-code did with a missing binary (mx-48140a). Mitigation: standard runVersionCheck flow already populates InstallCheckResult.path → SandboxProfile.toolchainPaths (mx-bd1445), and the devcontainer install in Step 6 covers the canonical Linux dev environment.

Headless subscription auth (ChatGPT Plus / Claude Pro / GitHub Copilot via /login) only works in pi's interactive mode and writes credentials under ~/.pi that the sandbox doesn't forward. On macOS pi may eventually move to Keychain like claude-code did (mx-d15efa). Mitigation: V1 is API-key-only (env-var auth, no headless OAuth) — sidestep the platform-specific credential channels entirely; record an explicit decision and file a follow-up seed pointing at mx-d15efa for the eventual subscription-auth work.

Forwarding OPENAI_API_KEY / GEMINI_API_KEY by default would leak user secrets into a sandbox running --provider anthropic. Mitigation: V1 envPassthrough is only the three ANTHROPIC_* names; users overriding --provider via burrow.toml [env] passthrough opt in explicitly per project (mx-d46d5d).

Pi's stdin-close exit semantics differ between providers: some upstream providers may keep the agent loop open waiting on RPC commands after the first prompt completes, which would hang the run-per-process model. Mitigation: Step 1's captured trace confirms agent_end + exit on stdin close for the pinned (anthropic, --no-session) combination; the handshake test from Step 7 locks the assumption.
acceptance: `pi` is installed in the .devcontainer image at v0.74.0 and `burrow doctor --agent pi` (or equivalent install probe) reports installed: true with a non-empty version string and a resolved binary path.

`burrow up --agent pi <project>` followed by `burrow prompt <id> "say hi"` produces at least one `text` event and exits 0 when ANTHROPIC_API_KEY is set on the host — exercised end-to-end inside the devcontainer.

piRuntime.buildSpawnCommand argv contains exactly `["pi","--mode","rpc","--no-session","--no-extensions","--provider","anthropic", ...]` — locked by a unit test that fails if any of those flags drops.

parsePiEvents maps every event type documented in pi's RPC spec (agent_start/end, turn_start/end, message_start/update/end, tool_execution_start/update/end, queue_update, compaction_start/end, auto_retry_start/end, extension_error, response) to a burrow RuntimeEvent without throwing — covered by per-envelope tests in src/runtime/parsers/pi.test.ts.
The golden handshake fixture test passes against the pi v0.74.0 stdout trace captured during Step 1 research; checked-in fixture and golden expectation are both under version control.
`burrow up --agent pi` with ANTHROPIC_API_KEY set on the host results in that env var being readable by the sandboxed process (existing envPassthrough integration coverage extended to include pi).
AgentRegistry boots with four built-ins; `new AgentRegistry().has("pi")` is true and `require("pi").displayName === "Pi"`.
SPEC.md §14.1 lists pi runtime under a kind-collapse footnote; CHANGELOG entry describes the addition and the pinned upstream version.
`bun test && bun run lint && bun run typecheck` pass cleanly with no warnings (Biome's --error-on-warnings posture).
burrow-8aff is closed with a pointer to this plan, plus three filed follow-up seeds (resume, mid-run steering, event-kind promotion).

sections.context: Warren's R-19 (per-run preview environments, plan pl-2c59, root seed warren-1bcb) requires two capabilities burrow doesn't expose today: inbound networking (bind a host loopback port and surface its traffic inside the sandbox's network namespace) and a sidecar exec endpoint (spawn preview.command as a long-lived process alongside a terminated agent run in the same workspace). The contract is locked in burrow SPEC §8.7 (2026-05-14), ROADMAP R-08, and warren SPEC §11.L. Coordination seed is burrow-8647; warren-side consumer is warren-83dc. This plan decomposes the burrow-side implementation into shipable steps so warren-side mirror work (HttpClient.sidecars on the warren burrow-client facade) can start against the locked contract while burrow's runtime layer lands.
sections.approach: Phase by the natural seam — contract-only first (so warren-side mirror can build against a stable shape), then Linux runtime (the canonical platform), then the sidecar HTTP namespace + HttpClient, then lifecycle wiring. Reuse existing burrow patterns: pure-profile-render-canonicalize-in-dispatcher (mx-9ce2b2) for the bwrap profile builder, http-server-skeleton (mx-d55f59) for the routes, http-workspace-files-namespace (mx-244cb2) shape for the HttpClient.sidecars namespace, workspace-file-seed-rollback (mx-bd72cf) for atomic seed-then-spawn. The Linux userspace forwarder uses Bun.listen + nsenter into /proc/<pid>/ns/net rather than adding socat as a runtime dependency — same posture as the restricted-network outbound proxy (mx-d6a44f). The macOS path is a no-op skip (sandbox-exec doesn't isolate the network namespace; warren acceptance scenario 20 documents the skip via mx-1d31f0). Cross-host placement (burrow-62ce [workers]) is explicitly out of scope: warren returns 501 on cross-host preview proxying (warren §11.L R-12 deferral), so the forward is local-to-the-worker.

alternatives.name: Add socat to the Docker image and shell out to it for forwarding
alternatives.rejected_because: Adds an unmanaged runtime dependency to every burrow image. Bun.listen + an explicit nsenter into /proc/<pid>/ns/net reuses the restricted-proxy posture already shipped (mx-d6a44f) without bloating the image or introducing a second process-supervision pattern.

alternatives.name: Collapse sidecars into the runs namespace with a 'kind' discriminator
alternatives.rejected_because: Runs and sidecars have different lifecycles (terminal stop_reason vs. long-lived), different event shapes (run_event vs. sidecar_*), and different terminal conditions. Half the columns on a unified row would be NULL for one kind; the wire envelope would either be polymorphic-via-discriminator (annoying to consume) or wrap both in a third envelope (worse than the route split). Keep them parallel namespaces.

alternatives.name: Make the sidecar cap a global server-wide ceiling rather than per-burrow
alternatives.rejected_because: Per-burrow is the right blast radius — one misbehaving warren run shouldn't bottleneck other burrows on the same worker. The cap of 4 sidecars per burrow plus an implicit ceiling from the host's available loopback port range is enough; revisit only when a real workload shows multi-sidecar-per-burrow density.

alternatives.name: Use a bwrap --bind-data trick to expose host-side sockets directly into the sandbox
alternatives.rejected_because: bwrap doesn't have a primitive for inbound network forwarding; --bind-data only exposes file content. The userspace forwarder is the only path that works without modifying the kernel netns rules from inside the sandbox, and it keeps the policy boundary explicit (burrow enforces; warren declares).

steps.title: SandboxProfile.inboundPortForwards type + provider plumbing (no forwarder yet)
Linux userspace forwarder (Bun.listen + nsenter pipe) — end-to-end test on Linux
POST /burrows/:id/sidecars + GET / DELETE / logs routes + OpenAPI golden + per-burrow cap
HttpClient.sidecars.* namespace surface on @os-eco/burrow
Sidecar lifecycle wiring: process-exit observer, cascade on burrow delete, sidecar_* events on the burrow event stream
Acceptance + warren-side cross-repo verification: warren acceptance scenario 20 runs against this burrow on Linux

risks: nsenter from a sibling process needs CAP_SYS_ADMIN; in-pod deploys without --cap-add SYS_ADMIN (already required for bwrap userns nesting per DEPLOY.md) silently fail to forward. Mitigation: the per-burrow forwarder logs a clear 'capability missing' error on startup and warren-side launch returns a typed error rather than hanging.

Sidecar inherits the parent burrow's network policy. If the burrow was up'd with network: 'none', preview.command can't fetch anything — looks like 'warren broke the preview' but is policy-correct. Mitigation: document in SPEC §8.7 and the warren preview docs that previews require network: 'restricted' or 'open' on the parent burrow; doctor check warns on the mismatch.

Burrow restart loses in-memory sidecar process handles. Mitigation: persist sidecar rows in the burrow DB with the parent burrow's id; on startup, reap orphaned sidecar rows (no live pid) by marking them 'exited' with exit_code=null and emitting sidecar_orphaned events. Warren's eviction worker treats orphaned as 'evict and re-launch if still desired'.

Per-burrow cap collision with warren's port allocator: if warren keeps a port reserved but burrow already terminated the sidecar (process crashed), warren thinks the port is in use until reconciliation. Mitigation: GET /burrows/:id/sidecars returns terminal sidecars too (state: exited/failed) for a configurable retention window; warren's eviction worker uses this as the source of truth and reclaims the port.
acceptance: SandboxProfile.inboundPortForwards type is shipped; bwrap profile builder accepts the field; sandbox-exec path documents the no-op skip; type-only tests pass.

Linux end-to-end test: a process bound to 127.0.0.1:3000 inside the sandbox is reachable at 127.0.0.1:<hostPort> on the host; bound to loopback only (assertion: a separate test confirms 0.0.0.0:<hostPort> from a different host interface 401s or is refused).
macOS integration test asserts host_port_bound: true is returned from sidecar create without spawning a forwarder, and warren acceptance scenario 20's macOS skip is documented.

POST /burrows/:id/sidecars (+ GET, DELETE, logs) ship; OpenAPI golden test in src/server/openapi/__golden__/openapi.json is updated; per-burrow cap (default 4, BURROW_SIDECAR_CAP override) enforced with 409 sidecar_cap_exceeded.
